findings.description: Full card number (PAN) and CVV are written in plaintext to the application log, a flagrant PCI-DSS 3.2/3.4 violation exposing cardholder data to anyone with log access.
charge_order has no idempotency or status precondition, so calling it twice on the same order double-charges the customer.
If the gateway charge succeeds but the subsequent UPDATE/commit fails, the customer is charged while the order is never marked paid, with no compensating rollback.
monthly_revenue_report issues one item query per paid order (N+1), which becomes thousands of round-trips at scale instead of a single JOIN/aggregate.
Monetary totals use binary float arithmetic (and the final total is left unrounded before int(round(total*100))), risking cent-level errors versus Decimal/integer-cents.
discount_pct is never bounds-checked: a value >100 yields a negative line total and a negative value inflates the charge.
No exception handling around DB connect, gateway charge, or commit; any sqlite/SDK error propagates raw instead of degrading gracefully.
charge accepts card_number/cvv with no null or format validation; a None card_number raises TypeError at card_number[-4:] on line 38.
Transaction id derived from Python hash() of card last-4 and amount is non-cryptographic, low-entropy, and collision-prone for a payment identifier.
discount = order['discount_pct'] or 0.0 coerces a legitimate stored 0 the same as NULL, masking any distinction between 'no discount' and 'explicit zero'.
api_key is stored as a plain attribute and is at risk of incidental exposure via logging/repr; no secret-handling discipline.
unit_price modeled as float rather than Decimal/integer cents is a money-handling design smell.
